narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students who’ve walked this path before. Your Year 3 decile of 4 stood out as the biggest chapter pulling the narrative toward concern—it suggested that, midway through law school, your grades placed you in the lower half of your class, a spot where many struggle to build the momentum needed for the bar exam. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 5, reinforcing a sense that early challenges in keeping pace with peers carried over, making the path ahead look steeper. Your upper-middle family income added another layer, as the model has seen this bracket sometimes correlate with added pressures that don't always translate to bar success in the way other backgrounds might. Your LSAT score of 33, while solid for many, didn't quite signal the exceptional firepower needed to overcome those grade trends in this prediction. Being Native American brought in historical patterns the model weighs heavily, often tied to systemic hurdles that have tripped up others on the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 echoed those earlier academic hurdles, painting a picture of consistency in needing more support to thrive at this level. Even as a full-time student and male, those factors nudged the story further toward risk, with the model seeing subtle patterns there too. Taken together, these elements wove a tale where the odds of bar passage tipped to 44% failure—not a done deal, but enough for the team to pause on your application. It's not about your worth or potential; it's about spotting these patterns early to guide everyone toward the best fit. With focused steps like grade-boosting strategies or bar prep immersion, you could rewrite this story—many have. We're rooting for you to turn the page.
